hero.line1: Luma didn't build an event platform.
hero.subheadline: How a calendar tool became the OS for startup communities — and why that distinction is worth a billion dollars.

core_insight.title: Core Insight
core_insight.statement: People attend events only if it gives them a sense of belonging.
core_insight.narrative: Eventbrite solved logistics. Partiful solved social coordination. Luma solved something deeper: the signal problem. In a world drowning with events, a Luma invite became a proxy for quality. The platform didn't manage RSVPs — it curated belonging.

timeline.title: How a weekend project became the backbone of startup culture.

events.heading: The itch they had to scratch
events.body: Co-founders Danqing Liu and Victor Pontis wanted to help a friend host yoga classes online, but the existing tools were too clunky. So they built a sleek platform to manage Zoom invites.

events.heading: MVP: a beautiful RSVP page
events.body: A simple, elegant landing-page builder with seamless Zoom integration — RSVPs, payments, and automated reminders.

events.heading: Seed round: $3M
events.body: Luma raised $3M, framed around helping businesses build on Zoom-era event workflows.

events.heading: Capturing recurring communities
events.body: Memberships, subscription tiers, member-only events and newsletters — a push toward recurring community infrastructure.

events.heading: Discovery, waitlists and moderation
events.body: Better discovery, search, notifications, paid-event waitlists, and block/report tools — a full event ecosystem.

product_loop.title: The product loop
product_loop.caption: This isn't a feature loop — it's a trust-compounding engine. Each revolution adds more signal, more density, and more reasons for the next host to choose Luma.

funding.title: Raising less, doing more
funding.narrative: Luma's capital strategy is deliberately lean. Free for free events; ~2–3% plus a small flat fee on paid tickets. You only pay when you earn — which self-selects for serious, recurring hosts.

rounds.label: Series A
rounds.signal: Funding the shift from tool to community OS.

funding.pricing_note: Free tier = zero friction to start. By the time you're charging tickets, your community already knows Luma — so the fee feels tiny against the credibility and infrastructure.

competitors.title: A crowded room with one clear winner
competitors.framing: Mapped on design quality (Y) vs community depth (X), only one platform nails both.

founder_mode.title: How they're organized internally
founder_mode.narrative: 6 people. Millions of users. No silos: designers do product + graphic, engineers code across web, iOS, Android, and API. Only two recurring meetings. Everything goes into the product.

lessons.headline: "Solve the host first. The audience follows."
lessons.body: Two-sided markets fail when they grow both sides at once. Go deep on one side and let the other materialize naturally.

lessons.headline: "Recurring communities compound. One-off needs don't."
lessons.body: Build for recurring behaviours, not one-time needs. Design the recurrence into the default experience.

lessons.headline: "Design is a filter, not just a feature."
lessons.body: High-quality design self-selects for high-quality users. You're communicating who this is for — and attracting exactly them.

closing.title: The NBT take
closing.narrative: Luma succeeded not because they built better event software, but because they leveraged a simple truth: in a networked world, participation is currency. They became the ledger — a very different, much more defensible business.
closing.pull_quote: The best startups don't just solve problems. They build psychological comfort and ways for users to create and share value.
